# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write Envoy proxy configuration for HTTP traffic routing. Define listeners on port 80, clusters for backend services, and routes to match requests. Configure connection pooling and health checks for upstream services. This is for our API gateway proxy.
# Category: envoy_no_tls

envoy_proxy:
  listeners:
    - name: listener_0
      address: any
      port: 80 # Listening on this port will allow traffic to the Envoy Proxy instance from anywhere.
  
  clusters:
     cluster1:
       socket_addresses:
         - endpoint { ip_address: "<backend-service-ip>", port_value : <port>  } # Cluster for backend service instances, replace '<...>' with your actual values (e.g., server address or IP)  and the corresponding services ports here as well e.g `server1`
       connect_timeout: "5s"
     cluster2:
      socket_addresses: # List of upstream clusters that our Envoy Proxy will route traffic to, you can add more if needed (optional)  Here are example nodes for server 'test-service' and port `301`. This is used as an inbuilt health check mechanism via stats dumps
        - endpoint { ip_address: "<upstream service address>", port_value : <port>  } # Server IP, replace with your actual value (e.g., server-ip) and corresponding services ports here e.g `server2`  or test1 for testing only as upstream node
       lb_policy: round_robin   # Load Balancing policy used by Envoy Proxy to distribute traffic among the nodes in our Cluster (optional, can be either 'round-robin' , 'leastconn', etc.). default is Round Robin if not specified.  For this example I have kept it as `RoundRobin`
     cluster3: # Health check mechanism configuration for backend service instances which are being routed by the Envoy Proxy, here we use port_values from server1 and add more nodes (optional) Here's an exmaple of node test2.  For this example only one endpoint has been included
        - address: { ip_address : "<health check backend service IP>",port_value:"305" } # Backend Service Ip, replace with your actual value (e.g., server-ip) and corresponding services ports here e.g `server1` or test2 for testing only as the healthcheck node
         connect_timeout: "6s"# Connection timeout after which Envoy Proxy will consider that a service is not healthy, replace '<port>' with your actual value (e.g., port number) and here we use 305 to represent `test2` or backend services health check
       retry_on: "health_check" # If the node returns an error other than HTTP/1 stream level errors, Envoy Proxy will wait for a specific duration (in seconds), before considering that instance as unhealthy. Here we use it to set 6s and replace '<port>' with your actual value
         health_checks: [ # list of configurations per backend service instances being checked by the load balancer, here are example stats dumps for server1 port `302` & test4 ip address. For this only one entry has been included  Here it is used as a check mechanism via sstats dump
           - interval : "5s" # How often to run health checks in seconds (optional), default: none if not specified, and runs every time Envoy Proxy processes an internal request within the cluster. This means you will want this set higher than your client read timeout for a smoother load balancing experience
             healthy_threshold : 2 # How many times to retry before considering it failed (optional), default: none if not specified, and tries until Envoy Proxy processes an internal request within the cluster. This means you can specify health checks that should fail for a certain number of requests or no further retries after which they are considered unhealthy
             timeout : "1s" # How long to wait before giving up when sending http/streaming responses (optional), default: none if not specified, and times out Envoy Proxy's internal request within the cluster. This means you can specify health checks that should fail for a certain number of requests or no further retries after which they are considered unhealthy
             target : "ip_address" # Whether to use 'uri', ip address (default), etc., as identifiers in http/streaming responses and headers, default: ipset.  For this example I have kept it set like that for `server1` or test2 IPs if required otherwise
    route-configs:
      - match: "prefix" # This will be replaced by Envoy with the common prefix to use when defining routes and targets, default is none (this means no routing)  Here I have set it as 'localhost' only for this example. It can also include a hostname or full fqdn etc if required
        route: [ # List of defined paths on which Envoy should react during traffic matching in the cluster to routes, here we use localhost that is our API gateway and port `80` (this means all requests from any location within this instance will be redirected elsewhere via HTTP)  
          - match: "localhost"  # This defines what paths on which Envoy should react during traffic matching in the cluster to routes, here it's a wildcard '*', meaning that everything else is matched and then all requests made from these hosts are forwarded where they can be routed by your backend services.
            route: [# Route Configuration for Cluster1  if required otherwise removed entirely])   # List of defined paths on which Envoy should react during traffic matching in the cluster to routes, here we use port `80` (this means all requests from any location within this instance will be redirected elsewhere via HTTP)
            target_clusters: [cluster1]  # This defines what clusters that matches with these paths on which Envoy should react during traffic matching in the cluster to routes, here it's only `Cluster` named 'Cluser', meaning you can use this as a source or upstream node for your health checks
      - match: "prefix"   # Route Configuration if required otherwise removed entirely  Here we have not defined any prefix (all paths will be matched by the router) because no specific routing rule has been provided. This could potentially cause confusion in case of multiple clusters being reached at once, depending on how Envoy's internal request is handled
        route: [ # List Of Routes that include `localhost` for all requests from any location within this instance (allows http/streaming responses to be forwarded elsewhere via HTTP)  Here we use localhost and port '80', meaning All Requests From Any Location Within This Instance Will Be Forwarded Elsewhere Via HttpOrStream
          - match: "localhost" # Match all requests made from these hosts in your clusters (allows http/streaming responses to be forwarded elsewhere via HTTP)  Here we use localhost and port '80' for both entries if required otherwise removed entirely ]   This could potentially cause confusion. All Requests From Any Location Within The Instance Will Be Forwarded Elsewhere Via HttpOrStream
          target_clusters: [cluster2] # Define what clusters that matches with these paths on which Envoy should react during traffic matching in the cluster to routes, here it's only `Cluster` named 'Cluser', meaning you can use this as a source or upstream node for your health checks
  stats_durations: ["5s", "6s"] # Stats dumps duration that will be used when sending http/streaming responses to the client, here we have set it like `['302', 'localhost']` and [`'1m','test4'], if required otherwise removed entirely
